conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

Before I can assess your eligibility, I need to gather some information about your circumstances. Let me start by asking:

Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children - Alex, who is 18, and Blake, who is 13. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can help you determine eligibility for Alex (18) and Blake (13).

Let me start gathering some important details. First:

Is Alex currently in full-time approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. They finished school and haven't continued with any courses.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

Now, regarding Blake who is 13 - does Blake live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake does spend time at home with me, but they're actually in care at the moment. They come home regularly though - they stay with me on weekends and some evenings during the week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I need to understand Blake's situation a bit more.

When Blake is in care, is this a local authority care arrangement? And approximately how many nights per week does Blake stay with you at home?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's a local authority care arrangement. Blake stays with me probably about 3 or 4 nights a week - most weekends and sometimes during the week as well.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to understand a bit more about the care arrangement.

Is Blake in care under a full Care Order, or is it a voluntary arrangement where you still have parental responsibility?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's a full care arrangement with the local authority - they have the care order. But Blake still comes home to stay with me regularly like I mentioned.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have one more important question about Blake's situation:

When Blake stays with you for those 3-4 nights per week, is this arrangement expected to continue for at least the next 8 weeks, or is it a temporary arrangement?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's an ongoing arrangement that's been in place for a while now and will definitely continue for the foreseeable future - well beyond 8 weeks.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me just read the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information about your eligibility.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Based on the information you've provided and the official guidance, I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Summary of Your Child Benefit Eligibility:**

**For Alex (18):** Unfortunately, you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit. Since Alex is over 16 and is not in approved education or training, they don't meet the eligibility criteria.

**For Blake (13):** You **are eligible** for Child Benefit. Even though Blake is in local authority care, the fact that Blake stays with you for 3-4 nights per week (which is well over 24 hours per week) means you meet the exception for children in care. This ongoing arrangement qualifies you to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

If you haven't already claimed for Blake, you should be able to apply. If you need any further assistance or have questions about making a claim, you can contact the Child Benefit Office directly.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 13 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child in care for 16 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child in care for 16 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and is not in full-time approved education or training. Under Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for a child under 16, OR for a child aged 16-19 if they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex has finished school and is not continuing with any courses or training, they do not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

Blake is 13 years old and is in local authority care under a full Care Order. However, Blake spends 3-4 nights per week staying with you at home, and this arrangement has been ongoing and will continue for well beyond 8 weeks. According to the rules for children in care, there is an exception: you may be able to claim Child Benefit if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home. With Blake staying 3-4 nights per week, this clearly exceeds 24 hours per week, so you meet the exception criteria and should be eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (18) and Blake (13). Alex is not eligible because they are over 16 and not in approved education or training. Blake is in local authority care but spends 3-4 nights per week (well over 24 hours) at home with the claimant on an ongoing basis, which meets the exception for children in care, making Blake eligible for Child Benefit.
